title: const — your go-to variable
instruction:
In Chapter 1 you typed `"JavaScript is awesome!"` directly in console.log. What if you need that value in ten places?

`const` creates a named container. Write it once, use it everywhere:

```js
const language = "JavaScript";
console.log(language);
```

Run this. Then look at the two console.log calls — they both use the same variable.
successMessage: One variable, used three times. Change 'language' in one place and all three outputs update. That's the entire point of variables.

title: Create your own const
instruction:
Create two `const` variables — `name` with your name and `city` with your city. Then print them with a template literal.

Remember: text needs quotes, numbers don't. `const age = 25` not `const age = "25"`.
starter: // TODO: create name and city constants, then print them

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: const is the right default for variables that don't need to change. Use it for everything — then switch to let only if you actually need to reassign.

title: const cannot be reassigned
instruction:
The 'const' in `const` stands for constant. Once set, you cannot reassign it.

Run this — it will crash with a `TypeError`. Then comment out the broken line so the rest runs.
hint: Put // in front of the language = "Python" line.
successMessage: TypeError: Assignment to constant variable. const protects you from accidental overwrites. When you see const, you know that value will never change — that's a guarantee.

title: let — when you need to change
instruction:
`let` works like `const` but allows reassignment. Use it when the value will genuinely change — like a score, a counter, or a status.

The score starts at 0 and increases. `const` would crash here — `let` is the right tool.
successMessage: let for things that change, const for things that don't. The rule: start with const. If JavaScript complains about reassignment, switch to let. Simple.

title: ++ and -- shortcuts
instruction:
Adding or subtracting 1 is so common JavaScript has special shortcuts:

- `count++` — increment by 1 (same as `count += 1`)
- `count--` — decrement by 1

Add `count++` three times and print after each, then `count--` once.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: count++ is everywhere — loop counters, score trackers, pagination. Small shortcut, used thousands of times.

title: var — and why to avoid it
instruction:
`var` is the original JavaScript variable declaration from 1995. It has two problems:

1. **Function-scoped, not block-scoped** — a `var` inside an `if` leaks outside it
2. **Hoisted** — you can use a `var` before you declare it (it's just `undefined`)

Run this to see both problems. Modern JavaScript uses `let` and `const` exclusively — `var` is legacy.
successMessage: var's block-scope leak is the source of countless JavaScript bugs. Modern JS developers never use var — const and let were specifically created to fix these problems.

title: Naming rules
instruction:
Variable names have rules:

- Only letters, digits, `_`, and `$`
- Cannot start with a digit
- Case-sensitive: `score` ≠ `Score`
- Convention: `camelCase` for variables (not `snake_case`)

Three names below are illegal. Fix them all.
hint: 1stPlace → firstPlace, full-name → fullName, user score → userScore
successMessage: camelCase is JavaScript's convention. firstNameLastName, getUserById, isLoggedIn — you'll write thousands of variable names. camelCase all of them.

title: Multiple assignments
instruction:
You can declare multiple variables on separate lines or use destructuring to unpack from an array (more on that in Chapter 11).

For now, declare three variables and then swap two of them.

To swap without a third variable use destructuring:
```js
[a, b] = [b, a];
```

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: [first, last] = [last, first] — one line, no temp variable. This is JavaScript destructuring and it's beautiful. Full chapter on it coming in Chapter 11.

title: undefined and the temporal dead zone
instruction:
An uninitialized `let` is `undefined`. Accessing a `let` before its declaration (the temporal dead zone) crashes.

This is `let` being stricter than `var` — on purpose:

```js
console.log(x);  // crashes — TDZ
let x = 5;
```

Fix the code so the variable is declared before it's used.
hint: Move the let declaration before the console.log.

codeChecks.message: Declare let username before using it.

successMessage: Declare before use — always. The TDZ exists to catch exactly this mistake. var would silently print undefined here, hiding the bug. let protects you.

title: Build a profile
instruction:
No starter code. Write everything from scratch.

Create these variables:
- `const name` — your name
- `const language` — `"JavaScript"`
- `let chaptersCompleted` — start at 0, then set it to 2
- `const isLearning` — `true`

Print all four. Then print a summary using a template literal: `"Alex is learning JavaScript — 2 chapters in!"`
hint: Mix const and let appropriately. Template literal: `${name} is learning ${language}...`

codeChecks.message: Use let (not const) for chaptersCompleted — it changes.

successMessage: const for stable data, let for changing data. That rule — applied consistently — prevents more bugs than almost anything else in JavaScript. Chapter 2 done!
